lines.text: I'm on the East Side near the Schenk's Corners intersection. Madison-wide we're at 139 kills, cumulative. That's 17 kills in the past hour — down from the 22 we saw in hour six. YoY at this point last year we were at 122. We're running 14% hot.

Holden, you were asking about the caller pattern. I'm seeing something odd in the real-time data — phone lines are jammed. We've had, what, Holden — how many texts did the board log in the last sixty minutes?
Approximately 340 text-ins. That's triple the average for this hour. Last year at 02:00 we had 110.

Right. So people aren't staying silent tonight. They're staying home *and* they're reaching out. That's a behavioral shift. The kill count doesn't reflect it — we're still tracking the cumulative pattern — but the psychology is different. Atwood Avenue behind me is quiet. Too quiet. Activity is concentrating on the corridor, not the residential blocks.

Weapons load has shifted. We're seeing 41% firearms, down from 52% last hour. Bladed and impact weapons up. Economic pressure hypothesis holding — people are rationing ammunition or they've switched tactics. The cold front's keeping people indoors or moving them to vehicle-based confrontations where firearms are less useful.

Holden, do you want me to reposition to the Beltline corridor or stay on residential perimeter? I'm picking up some movement on Atwood that — hold on, my body-cam just — the battery indicator is — [CUT OFF]

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 139 cumulative kills. +14% vs. 2168 same hour (122). East Side accounting for 22% of total activity.
Text-in volume surge: 340 messages in hour 8 vs. 110 YoY. Phone lines busier than any prior Purge on record.
Atwood Avenue / Schenk's Corners residential: quiet. Activity concentrating on Beltline corridor and East Washington commercial strip.
Someone named 'J' texted: 'My neighbor just tried the door three times. He's still out there. How long until 7am?' — Tim flagged for follow-up.
